summary: Every sales row can introduce a new date-and-make group, lead, or partner, so exact grouped distinct counts have a linear inspection lower bound; one hash-aggregation scan matches it.

optimality.argument: Changing any uninspected row can create a new output group or increase either distinct count, so every one of the R rows must be considered in the worst case. Hashing each row into its date-and-make group and inserting its two bounded identifiers into group-local sets takes expected O(1) work per row and O(R) total time.

replacement_checks.evidence: An adversary can make the final uninspected row the only row for a new group, the only new lead, or the only new partner, changing the required table.
A single hash-aggregation pass maintains two distinct identifier sets per date-and-make key and emits one row per key without a required sort.
Authored fixtures cover the public sample, exact duplicate rows, one lead with several partners, one partner with several leads, separate makes on one date, and one make on separate dates.
